input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-11T01:03:22.348Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user has migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their project files to understand the setup.

data.content: Confirmed — zero tests causes a failure. The fix is the `--minimum-expected-tests` option introduced by Microsoft.Testing.Platform. You can set it to `0` in the project file so it applies automatically.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Allow assemblies with zero tests (e.g. conditional compilation) to pass -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
I can see the issue. With Microsoft.Testing.Platform, when there are zero tests, it fails with exit code 1 (shown as "Failed! - Failed: 0, Passed: 0, Skipped: 0, Total: 0"). The fix is to set the MSBuild property `<TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>false</TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>` or more specifically, there's a property to ignore the "no tests" scenario.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The Microsoft.Testing.Platform has a specific exit code 8 for "no tests found". The user mentions exit code 8. In the `dotnet test` output we see exit code 1, but that might be because dotnet test wraps it.


The solution is to use the `--minimum-expected-tests 0` command-line option when running tests, or configure the equivalent MSBuild property to prevent the platform from treating zero tests as a failure condition.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Allow assemblies with zero tests (e.g. conditional compilation) to pass -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText: That didn't work. Let me check the log file for more details and try a different approach.

The command line shows `--minimum-expected-tests 0` is being passed. But the log seems to show the help output, which means the argument might not be recognized by this version. Let me check if the log shows an error, and also check what version of the testing platform is being used.

Looking at the log more closely, it seems like the file has a lot of null bytes (spaces between every character) which is a UTF-16 encoding. Let me try to read it differently.

Also, the log shows the command line ends with `--minimum-expected-tests 0` but the test is still failing. The fact it's showing the help text suggests the argument might be unrecognized or there's some other issue.


I should run the test executable directly with that flag to see what actually happens.

data.content: The `--minimum-expected-tests` doesn't accept `0`. But the log reveals the right option: `--ignore-exit-code 8` (exit code 8 = no tests found).

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Allow assemblies with zero tests (e.g. conditional compilation) to pass -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat "no tests discovered" (exit code 8) as success for conditional compilation scenarios -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
The error says `--minimum-expected-tests` expects a non-zero positive integer, so `0` is not accepted. But there's another option: `--ignore-exit-code` which can ignore specific exit codes. The exit code for "no tests" is 8. So we can use `--ignore-exit-code 8` instead.

Let me update the project file to use this approach.

<!-- Allow assemblies with zero tests (e.g. conditional compilation) to pass -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat "no tests discovered" (exit code 8) as success for conditional compilation scenarios -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.content:
**The fix:** Add this property to your `.csproj`:

```xml
<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
```

**What happened:** Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns exit code **8** when zero tests are discovered — unlike VSTest which treated that as success. The `--ignore-exit-code 8` flag maps that specific exit code back to `0`, restoring the VSTest behavior while still failing on real test failures.
